title: What Scripture actually says about self-control (it's not willpower)
caption:
Your self-control isn't the problem. Your root is.

Most men try to fix self-control the same way they'd fix a leaky faucet — tighten something, add more structure, push harder. It still falls apart at 11 PM.

But Scripture doesn't call self-control a skill you build. Paul calls it fruit — the last one listed in Galatians 5:22. You don't manufacture fruit. You grow in soil that produces it.

In 1 Corinthians 9, Paul trains like an athlete — disciplining his body, keeping it under control. The discipline is real. But the point isn't the regimen. The point is who he's accountable to.

The reason most men fail in private isn't a lack of willpower. It's that they're trying to win alone. Jesus didn't hedge it: "Apart from me you can do nothing." — John 15:5.

The practice that actually changes it isn't stricter rules. It's a deeper root. Word before the phone. Prayer before the noise of the day builds. One brother who can ask the question you've been avoiding.

Tend the root. The fruit follows.

—

Comment "roots" and I'll send you the link to ETHOS.

theme: He runs his life — his impulses don't control him.
